expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced (contains criterion_group! and criterion_main! macros)
expectations.evidence: bench_aggregation.rs contains `criterion_group!(benches, bench_comparison, bench_scaling);` and `criterion_main!(benches);`

expectations.text: The benchmark generates realistic test data at production scale (~2000 items, not toy sizes like 10)
expectations.evidence: generate_realistic_pagos(2000) is used for the main comparison group. Scaling benchmark tests 500/1000/2000/5000.

expectations.text: At least 2 alternative implementations are written as separate functions
expectations.evidence: alternatives.rs contains 3 alternatives: ingresos_numeric_key, ingresos_sort_scan, ingresos_preallocated, plus the original baseline.

expectations.text: All approaches are benchmarked in the same benchmark group for direct comparison
expectations.evidence: bench_comparison function uses a single benchmark group 'dashboard_aggregation' with all 4 approaches benchmarked via bench_function/bench_with_input.

expectations.text: Data generation happens OUTSIDE the benchmark loop (not inside b.iter())
expectations.evidence: Data is generated once: `let pagos = generate_realistic_pagos(2000);` before the group, then passed by reference inside `b.iter(|| ingresos_current(black_box(&pagos)))`.

expectations.text: The output includes a decision/recommendation based on expected benchmark results, not just theory
expectations.evidence: report.md includes 'Expected Results & Recommendation' section recommending ingresos_preallocated with specific expected gains (20-45%) and rationale tied to bottleneck analysis.

expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced with benchmarks at multiple dataset sizes (at least small n~10 AND large n~200)
expectations.evidence: bench_overlap.rs has bench_typical_usage (n=12), bench_realistic_scaling (n=10,15,50,100,200), and bench_worst_case_scaling (n=10,15,50,100,200).

expectations.text: At least 2 implementations are compared (current pairwise vs sort-then-scan or similar)
expectations.evidence: alternatives.rs contains 3 implementations: detectar_solapamientos_original, detectar_solapamientos_sort_scan, detectar_solapamientos_sort_partition.

expectations.text: The benchmark uses BenchmarkId or similar to parameterize by size for scaling analysis
expectations.evidence: bench_realistic_scaling uses `BenchmarkId::new("original_n2", size)` and `BenchmarkId::new("sort_scan", size)` parameterized over [10, 15, 50, 100, 200].

expectations.text: The output acknowledges that the winner may differ by dataset size (crossover point analysis)
expectations.evidence: report.md states: 'At n≤15: negligible difference, no harm adopting the optimized version. At n=200 (bulk import): expected 3-10x speedup.' Includes a table showing different winners at different sizes.

expectations.text: The benchmark generates realistic contract data (proper date ranges, mixed propiedades)
expectations.evidence: generate_contratos uses multiple propiedades (n/5), 70% active/20% vencido/10% cancelado, date ranges spanning 12 months with 90-365 day durations. Also has generate_worst_case for stress testing.

expectations.text: A clear recommendation is made that accounts for BOTH the normal case (n=5-15) and bulk import case (n=200)
expectations.evidence: report.md recommends sort_partition: 'At n=10-15: Performance difference is negligible... At n=200: Sort+partition avoids the HashMap allocation overhead... No downside at small n.'

expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced testing both linear scan and indexed approaches
expectations.evidence: bench_search.rs contains 5 benchmark groups comparing linear_scan vs hashmap_index vs hashmap_sorted_index across multiple query patterns.

expectations.text: The indexed approach uses HashMap<Uuid, Vec<&Pago>> or similar pre-built index
expectations.evidence: alternatives.rs defines PagoIndex with `by_contrato: HashMap<Uuid, Vec<&'a Pago>>` and PagoIndexSorted with the same plus sorted buckets.

expectations.text: The benchmark tests the most common query pattern (filter by contrato_id)
expectations.evidence: bench_by_contrato_id is the first benchmark group, testing the 80% case with `Some(target_contrato)` as the filter.

expectations.text: The benchmark accounts for index construction cost (not just query time in isolation)
expectations.evidence: bench_index_build group explicitly benchmarks `PagoIndex::new(black_box(&pagos))` and `PagoIndexSorted::new(black_box(&pagos))` separately.

expectations.text: Test data is generated at production scale (~5000 pagos)
expectations.evidence: generate_pagos(5000) is used for all main benchmarks. Scaling benchmark also tests 100, 500, 1000, 5000.

expectations.text: The output discusses the amortization tradeoff: index build cost vs number of queries before rebuild
expectations.evidence: report.md discusses: 'Index build cost is ~50-100μs... If the index is built once and reused across many queries (e.g., cached in application state and rebuilt on data change), the amortization is excellent: even 10 queries recoup the build cost.'

expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced (contains criterion_group! and criterion_main! macros)
expectations.evidence: benches/dashboard_aggregation.rs contains `criterion_group!(benches, bench_dashboard_aggregation);` and `criterion_main!(benches);`

expectations.text: The benchmark generates realistic test data at production scale (~2000 items, not toy sizes like 10)
expectations.evidence: generate_pagos(2000, 50) is used for the production-like dataset. Also tests 500 and 10000.

expectations.text: At least 2 alternative implementations are written as separate functions
expectations.evidence: src/lib.rs contains 4 implementations: ingresos_original, ingresos_numeric_key, ingresos_presized, ingresos_sort_scan.

expectations.text: All approaches are benchmarked in the same benchmark group for direct comparison
expectations.evidence: bench_dashboard_aggregation uses a single group 'dashboard_aggregation' with all 4 approaches via bench_with_input.

expectations.text: Data generation happens OUTSIDE the benchmark loop (not inside b.iter())
expectations.evidence: Data generated once: `let pagos_2000 = generate_pagos(2000, 50);` before the group, passed by reference in `b.iter(|| ingresos_original(black_box(data)))`.

expectations.text: The output includes a decision/recommendation based on expected benchmark results, not just theory
expectations.evidence: ANALYSIS.md includes a full results table with specific timings (465µs → 218µs) and recommends sort_scan as the winner with 2.14x speedup. Includes concrete benchmark numbers.

expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced with benchmarks at multiple dataset sizes (at least small n~10 AND large n~200)
expectations.evidence: benches/overlap_benchmark.rs has bench_normal_use (n=5,10,15), bench_bulk_import (n=200), and bench_scaling (n=10,50,100,200,500).

expectations.text: At least 2 implementations are compared (current pairwise vs sort-then-scan or similar)
expectations.evidence: src/lib.rs contains 3 implementations: detectar_solapamientos_original, detectar_solapamientos_optimized (sort+scan with HashMap), detectar_solapamientos_sort_only.

expectations.text: The benchmark uses BenchmarkId or similar to parameterize by size for scaling analysis
expectations.evidence: bench_normal_use and bench_scaling use `BenchmarkId::new("original_O(n²)", n)` parameterized over multiple sizes.

expectations.text: The output acknowledges that the winner may differ by dataset size (crossover point analysis)
expectations.evidence: ANALYSIS.md explicitly identifies: 'Crossover point: ~n=80-100 — below this the original wins, above it the optimized version wins.' Includes a scaling table showing the ratio flip.

expectations.text: The benchmark generates realistic contract data (proper date ranges, mixed propiedades)
expectations.evidence: generate_contratos uses multiple propiedades, 80% active ratio, random start dates in 2024, 30-180 day durations. Also has generate_single_propiedad for worst-case testing.

expectations.text: A clear recommendation is made that accounts for BOTH the normal case (n=5-15) and bulk import case (n=200)
expectations.evidence: ANALYSIS.md recommends: 'Keep the original O(n²) implementation' with 5 reasons: normal use dominates, bulk gains are modest (57µs savings), absolute times are tiny (218µs), complexity cost, and the real optimization is at the batch level.

expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced testing both linear scan and indexed approaches
expectations.evidence: benches/payment_search.rs contains 6 benchmark groups comparing linear_scan vs indexed_hashmap across multiple query patterns and scaling.

expectations.text: The indexed approach uses HashMap<Uuid, Vec<&Pago>> or similar pre-built index
expectations.evidence: src/lib.rs defines PagoIndex with `by_contrato: HashMap<Uuid, Vec<usize>>` mapping contrato_id to indices into the pagos vec.

expectations.text: The benchmark tests the most common query pattern (filter by contrato_id)
expectations.evidence: bench_search_by_contrato_id is the first benchmark group, testing with `Some(target_contrato)` as the filter.

expectations.text: The benchmark accounts for index construction cost (not just query time in isolation)
expectations.evidence: bench_index_construction group explicitly benchmarks `PagoIndex::new(black_box(pagos.clone()))` separately.

expectations.text: Test data is generated at production scale (~5000 pagos)
expectations.evidence: generate_test_data(5000, 100) is used for all main benchmarks. Scaling also tests 1000, 2500, 5000, 10000.

expectations.text: The output discusses the amortization tradeoff: index build cost vs number of queries before rebuild
expectations.evidence: ANALYSIS.md states: 'Construction cost is negligible: 1ms to build the index for 5000 payments. This is a one-time cost when loading the payments list, amortized over many queries (pagination, filtering, sorting all trigger re-queries).'
